comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body: Evidence for AC 'make lint && make typecheck && make test pass': verified locally in the worktree — make lint clean (ruff check + format + zizmor + actionlint), make typecheck clean (mypy, 327 source files), and the full default suite green (6049 passed). Also verified by CI: Gerrit change 716 (https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/c/rebar/+/716) carries Verified+1, the CI gate that runs build/test/lint/typecheck on GitHub Actions across py3.11/3.12/3.13 + macOS.

compiled_state.description:
# S2 — Auth framework (composite audience choke point) + Resource-Server wiring + static verifier

## Why
This story delivers the authentication admission core — the provider-agnostic seam every verifier
plugs into, and the single audience/fail-closed choke point the whole security model rests on —
plus the first (dependency-free) verifier so the path is exercisable locally: the static bearer
token (the "PAT" half of the confirmed floor).

## What
- Extend the flat `McpConfig`/`_SECTIONS['mcp']` (from S1) with flat scalar auth keys:
  `auth_enabled` (bool, default false), `auth_strategies` (comma-separated → ordered list),
  `auth_issuer_url` (str), `auth_resource_server_url` (str — the SINGLE resource identifier),
  `auth_required_scopes` (comma-separated → list), and `auth_static_tokens_file` (str path). Each is
  one field + one flat coercer entry; register in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json` and regenerate
  `docs/mcp-reference.md`/`docs/env-vars.md`. There is **one** `auth_resource_server_url` for the
  whole server: it is what the RFC 9728 PRM advertises AND the audience every verifier is checked
  against — never a separate per-verifier audience. Per-verifier params (S3–S6) are additional flat
  keys those stories add; the flat schema holds **one instance per strategy type** (for multiple
  issuers, use the custom verifier) — documented in S7.
- New module `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`:
  - A `CompositeTokenVerifier` that is the **single audience + fail-closed choke point**. It tries
    each active verifier in order; a verifier returning an `AccessToken` is a candidate; a verifier
    returning `None` means "not my token" → try next; a verifier that **raises** is logged and
    treated as **non-acceptance** (never acceptance) → try next. For any candidate token the
    composite then **rejects unless `token.resource` is present AND equals `auth_resource_server_url`**
    (RFC 8707) — this re-check is independent of the sub-verifier, so no verifier (including a buggy
    or malicious custom one that returns `resource=None`) can bypass audience validation. If no
    verifier yields a valid, audience-matching token, the request is denied (401). An exception never
    yields acceptance.
  - A config→verifier **factory** built from `auth_strategies`, whose value vocabulary is the CLOSED
    set `static | jwt | introspection | proxy | custom`. An unrecognized entry is a **hard startup
    error**; `auth_enabled=true` producing an **empty** composite is a hard startup error; and
    `auth_enabled` with `transport=http` where no verifier is wired **refuses to start** (never a
    silent open endpoint).
  - The **`StaticBearerVerifier`**: reads the secrets file (below), stores only SHA-256 digests,
    compares with `hmac.compare_digest`, assigns each token's `client_id`/`scopes`, and stamps every
    returned `AccessToken` with `resource=auth_resource_server_url` so it passes the composite check.
- Wire it in `build_server`: when `auth_enabled`, pass `token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…)` and
  `auth=AuthSettings(issuer_url=auth_issuer_url, resource_server_url=auth_resource_server_url,
  required_scopes=auth_required_scopes)` to `FastMCP`, so the SDK serves the RFC 9728 PRM (a SINGLE
  `authorization_servers` entry derived from `issuer_url`) and emits 401 + `WWW-Authenticate`.
  Resolve S1's unauthenticated-HTTP guard against the real `auth_enabled`.
- **No-log redaction:** add a redaction helper used on every auth error/log path (composite raise
  handler, verifier errors) so no token/secret substring is ever emitted.

**Static-token secrets format (parsed by the verifier, NOT the TOML coercer).** The per-token
records live in the JSON/TOML file named by `auth_static_tokens_file`, parsed by the
`StaticBearerVerifier` at load — outside the flat coercer (which cannot hold variable-length records).
Each record supplies `client_id`, `scopes` (list), and the secret as EXACTLY ONE of: `token_env`
(the NAME of an env var holding the plaintext — preferred; nothing secret in the file), or
`token_sha256` (a precomputed lowercase SHA-256 hex digest, safe ONLY for a high-entropy token). The
verifier resolves plaintext from `token_env` and stores only its SHA-256; a record embedding a
plaintext literal, or missing both fields, is rejected at startup. Static tokens MUST be ≥128-bit
random; provide a `rebar` helper/documented one-liner to generate one (S7 documents the entropy
requirement and that static tokens are non-expiring, so `token_env` is preferred).

## Approach & rationale
Model the framework on the SDK's own `TokenVerifier`/`AuthSettings` seam (the blessed extension
point the `simple-auth` example uses), and make the composite the ONE place audience + fail-closed
are enforced, because the SDK's `BearerAuthBackend` never inspects `.resource` (grounded against
mcp 1.28.1) — there is no backstop behind the verifier. Keep config flat to fit `coerce_sparse`. A
composite (not a single selected strategy) lets a deployment accept static PAT for CI AND OIDC JWTs
for humans at once (the GitHub model). Static bearer first because it needs no external IdP, making
the end-to-end 401/200/403 path locally verifiable.

## Scope
The auth framework (composite choke point + factory + flat config + PRM/401 wiring + redaction) and
the **static** verifier only. JWKS/JWT (S3), introspection (S4), trusted-proxy (S5), custom (S6)
register additional verifiers on this seam. Subset of the epic. Depends on S1.

## Security
Boundary-crossing. Enforced here: the composite audience re-check (every accepted token's
`resource` == `auth_resource_server_url`); fail-closed (raise = non-acceptance, misconfig/unknown-
strategy/empty-composite/no-verifier-over-HTTP = refuse start, no verifier accepting = 401);
constant-time static compare; only SHA-256 digests held (no plaintext, no plaintext in config);
tokens/secrets never logged (redaction + test); a positive `expires_at` is set where a token has one
(never 0, which the SDK treats as no-expiry). Static tokens are dev/internal-grade, ≥128-bit, and
non-expiring (documented in S7).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The flat `auth_enabled`/`auth_strategies`/`auth_issuer_url`/`auth_resource_server_url`/
      `auth_required_scopes`/`auth_static_tokens_file` keys exist as `McpConfig` fields + flat
      coercer entries, appear in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`, and the regenerated reference docs +
      `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` pass (observable: manifest drift test green; a round-trip
      test reads each key from TOML and from its env var).
- [ ] Integration test (tier: integration — httpx `ASGITransport` against the SDK's Streamable-HTTP
      ASGI entrypoint) asserts, with static auth enabled: unauthenticated → **401** + a
      `WWW-Authenticate` header carrying the RFC 9728 `resource_metadata` URL (observable oracle:
      status + header value).
- [ ] Integration test asserts the PRM is served at `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/…` with a
      single `authorization_servers` entry (observable oracle: HTTP 200 + JSON field).
- [ ] Integration test asserts: valid static token → **200**; unknown/invalid token → 401; a
      principal lacking a `required_scope` → **403**; and a token whose `resource` ≠
      `auth_resource_server_url` (injected via a stub verifier in the composite) → 401 at the
      composite regardless of the sub-verifier (negative controls included).
- [ ] Integration test asserts session-id replay cannot hijack a principal: an `Mcp-Session-Id`
      obtained by one authenticated client, replayed by a DIFFERENT client that presents no valid
      token of its own, is NEVER served in the first principal's authenticated context (single
      observable oracle: the replayed request does not return the original principal's authorized
      access — it is denied 401 / treated as unauthenticated).
- [ ] Unit test asserts the composite contract: `None` → try next; an `AccessToken` with matching
      `resource` → accept; a verifier that **raises** → logged, non-acceptance, request denied (no
      swallow-to-accept), with a control where a raising verifier ordered after an accepting one is
      never consulted.
- [ ] Unit test asserts fail-closed startup: an unknown `auth_strategies` entry, an empty composite
      with `auth_enabled=true`, and `auth_enabled` + `transport=http` with no verifier wired each
      refuse to start (observable oracle: startup raises a clear message).
- [ ] Unit test asserts `StaticBearerVerifier` holds only SHA-256 digests (not plaintext), uses a
      constant-time compare, stamps `resource=auth_resource_server_url`, and rejects a secrets-file
      record with a plaintext literal or missing both `token_env`/`token_sha256`.
- [ ] Unit test asserts no token/secret substring appears in emitted logs across the composite
      raise-handler and a verifier error path (observable oracle: captured logs contain neither the
      token nor the secret).
- [ ] With `auth_enabled=false`, an integration test (with `allow_unauthenticated_http=true`) asserts
      no auth middleware: a no-token HTTP request gets a normal MCP response (not 401), and the stdio
      suite `tests/interfaces/facades/test_mcp.py` still passes unchanged.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.

## Compatibility
Additive; auth off by default. Only ADDS flat scalar keys to the existing flat `McpConfig`/
`_SECTIONS['mcp']` — no change to the coercer architecture, stdio, or the existing tools. Depends on
S1's transport + SDK pin.
compiled_state.title: S2 — Auth framework + Resource-Server wiring + static-bearer verifier
